narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 60.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction is based on your overall profile, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.8, which is below the typical 3.21 average; your LSAT score of 40, which is stronger than the average of 36.7; your upper-middle family income bracket; and your status as a full-time student, along with being female and White, both of which align closely with common distributions in our dataset. What stood out most to the model were your school decile rankings—both your Year 1 decile of 1 and Year 3 decile of 1, far below the averages of about 5.7 and 5.5—making them the top two factors influencing the decision toward predicting failure. Next in influence came your family income, followed by your undergraduate GPA, your LSAT score, your racial background, your gender, and finally your full-time enrollment status. Each of these elements nudged the prediction slightly toward expecting a bar failure, with your low early-year deciles having the strongest pull because they signal weaker academic performance compared to most applicants who rank in the middle of their classes. Your higher-than-average LSAT was a positive note but couldn't outweigh those early academic signals in the model's view. In comparison to typical students, your profile shows room for improvement mainly in grades and consistency, as many successful bar passers have stronger decile rankings and GPAs closer to or above 3.2. While demographics like gender and race played smaller roles and matched dataset norms, they didn't shift the outcome much. To boost your future chances, focus on building a stronger academic track record—perhaps by excelling in any remaining coursework or graduate prep programs to lift those decile perceptions. Retaking the LSAT to push your score even higher could help counterbalance past grades, since it's a changeable factor. Strengthening study habits or seeking tutoring tailored to bar exam demands might also address the patterns the model flagged. With targeted improvements like these, you could present a more competitive profile next time and tilt predictions in your favor. Keep pushing forward—you have solid strengths like your LSAT to build on.
